competitors.label: Angular Template Binding Guide
competitors.selectionReason: This is the official binding guide and the strongest canonical competitor for interpolation, property binding, attributes, and event binding.
competitors.winReason: Angular.dev is authoritative on syntax, but our page is more explicit about the production bugs caused by property-versus-attribute confusion and by overusing two-way binding.
theirStrengths: It precisely explains DOM properties, attributes, interpolation, and how Angular writes values into the DOM.
theirGaps: It is less centered on stale-UI debugging, attr-vs-property interview traps, and when two-way binding becomes a code smell.

realWorldUseCases: Most bindings are one-way in one direction at a time : component state flows into the DOM, events flow back into the component, and [(...)] is just sugar on top.
followUpConfusion: [disabled] sets the DOM property (actual runtime behavior). [attr.disabled] sets/removes the HTML attribute string.

realWorldUseCases: The official guide describes binding mechanics precisely, but it is less anchored to the concrete UI-debug mistakes our page foregrounds.
actionableExamples: Angular.dev already includes property and attribute binding examples, so the example comparison stays close.
followUpConfusion: The docs explain attr prefixes and property binding, but they do not push as hard on the overused two-way-binding and stale-UI pitfall framing.

actionableExamples: Add one before-and-after snippet where [attr.disabled] is wrong and [disabled] fixes a real bug.

competitors.label: GreatFrontEnd Angular Basic Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd’s Angular basics guide has a directly relevant data-binding section and is the right interview-prep comparator.
competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd explains the binding modes well, but our page is more specific about property-versus-attribute bugs and why two-way binding is just sugar, not a default mental model.
theirStrengths: It clearly enumerates one-way versus two-way data binding with familiar examples.
theirGaps: It is lighter on runtime binding bugs and on the property-versus-attribute trap.

realWorldUseCases: Most bindings are one-way in one direction at a time : component state flows into the DOM, events flow back into the component, and [(...)] is just sugar on top.
followUpConfusion: [disabled] sets the DOM property (actual runtime behavior). [attr.disabled] sets/removes the HTML attribute string.

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd covers the binding modes, but it does not push as hard on the production bug surface around property and attribute confusion.
actionableExamples: Its binding examples are concrete enough that the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: The guide does not spend much time on why [(...)] is only syntax sugar or on when attr.* is required.

actionableExamples: Add one quick ARIA binding example to reinforce when attr.* is the right tool.

competitors.label: Angular University Unidirectional Data Flow Guide
competitors.selectionReason: This is a strong reference competitor because it goes deep on Angular’s unidirectional rendering model and the reasoning behind expression-changed errors.
competitors.winReason: Angular University remains stronger as a long-form rendering article, but our page now makes the property-versus-attribute bug and the OnPush or signals follow-up much more explicit in one compact answer.
theirStrengths: It explains how Angular renders, why unidirectional data flow matters, and how expression-changed bugs happen.
theirGaps: It is broader on rendering theory than on day-to-day binding syntax choices.

realWorldUseCases: <!-- ❌ Wrong mental model: attribute binding for real control state -->
followUpConfusion: The one-way mental model matters even more with OnPush and signal-driven UIs.

realWorldUseCases: Angular University also ties binding behavior to real rendering and change-detection bugs, but our page now turns the wrong-binding-target problem into a more concrete control-state bug.
actionableExamples: Its rendering walkthrough and error-driven examples are still more extensive than our short trivia answer, so the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: It helps with the deeper change-detection follow-up, but our page now directly links the one-way model to OnPush, signals, and dev-versus-prod debugging.

actionableExamples: Add one compact example that contrasts property binding, attr binding, and two-way sugar in the same component.
